title: Topic 39: Cheating in Battle Royales: Why 100-Player Games Are Hacker Magnets
content:
# Topic 39: Cheating in Battle Royales: Why 100-Player Games Are Hacker Magnets

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the digital trenches of battle royale games, a genre where 100 players enter, but only one emerges victorious—often because they’re the one with the aimbot. We explore the curious phenomenon of cheating in these high-stakes virtual coliseums, examining why the allure of a digital chicken dinner drives a significant minority to the dark side of illicit software. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of psychological drivers, game design flaws, and the socio-economic landscape of the cheating underworld, we posit that battle royales are not merely games but petri dishes for questionable human behavior. This study combines scholarly research with a healthy dose of sarcasm to understand why, in a world of infinite respawns, so many choose to play dirty. We’ll uncover the motivations, from the desperate cry for validation to the sheer, unadulterated joy of being a troll. Ultimately, this paper will not solve cheating, but it will give you something to read while you’re waiting for your account to be unbanned.

## INTRODUCTION

Picture the scene: you’ve survived the initial chaos of the drop, you’re geared up with a level-three helmet and a sniper rifle that could take the head off a gnat at 500 paces. You’ve outsmarted, outrun, and outgunned 98 other hopefuls. It’s just you and one other player left. The circle is closing, your palms are sweating, and your heart is pounding a rhythm that would make a drum and bass DJ proud. You peek over the ridge, line up the shot of a lifetime, and… you’re dead. Not just dead, but obliterated by a player who was simultaneously jumping, spinning 360 degrees, and firing a pistol from the other side of the map. Welcome to the modern battle royale experience, where the line between elite skill and illicit software is often blurrier than a Monet painting in a hurricane.

Battle royale games, the digital equivalent of a gladiatorial free-for-all, have become a cultural phenomenon. Titles like *Fortnite*, *PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds (PUBG)*, and *Apex Legends* have amassed hundreds of millions of players, all vying for that coveted top spot. The premise is simple: a large number of players are dropped into a large map, the playable area shrinks over time, and the last one standing wins. It’s a high-stakes, high-adrenaline formula that has proven to be wildly successful. However, this very design, the one-versus-ninety-nine struggle for supremacy, has also turned these games into a veritable magnet for cheaters. The massive player count, the allure of victory, and the anonymity of the internet create a perfect storm for those who would rather download their skill than earn it.

This paper will explore the multifaceted world of cheating in battle royales. We will investigate the psychological underpinnings that motivate players to cheat, from a desperate need for validation to a simple, malicious desire to watch the world burn. We will also examine the game design elements that make battle royales particularly susceptible to hacking, and the burgeoning black market that supplies these digital ne’er-do-wells with their tools of the trade. Through a combination of academic research, real-world examples, and a liberal application of gallows humor, we will attempt to understand why, in a game with 99 opponents, the biggest threat is often the one who isn’t playing by the rules.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic inquiry into video game cheating is a field as old as the Konami Code and twice as convoluted. Scholars, in their infinite wisdom, have traded their dusty libraries for the neon-lit, Dorito-dusted basements of the digital age to answer one profound question: why do people cheat? The literature reveals a fascinating, if slightly depressing, tapestry of human motivation, stretching from the hallowed halls of psychology to the sticky floors of online forums.

Early research into cheating often focused on single-player games, where the act was seen as a relatively benign way to overcome challenges or simply to experience the game differently. The seminal, though entirely fictional, work of Von Stroheim (1986) on “God Mode and Its Discontents” argued that cheating was a form of player empowerment, a way to subvert the developer's intended narrative and create one's own. However, the advent of multiplayer gaming, and particularly the high-stakes environment of battle royales, transformed cheating from a personal choice into a public menace. As one researcher eloquently put it, “Your god mode is my game over screen” (A. Nonymous, personal communication, 2023).

A significant body of research points to psychological drivers. Lee et al. (2021, 2023) have extensively studied the role of self-determination theory, suggesting that players who feel their needs for competence, autonomy, and relatedness are not being met are more likely to cheat. In layman's terms, if you’re bad at the game (competence), feel forced to play a certain way (autonomy), and have no friends (relatedness), you’re a prime candidate for downloading an aimbot. This is the “I can’t win, so I’ll buy a win” school of thought, a digital manifestation of keeping up with the Joneses, if the Joneses had wallhacks and unlimited ammo.

Other studies delve into the darker corners of the human psyche. Research has linked cheating to the “dark triad” of personality traits: narcissism, Machiavellianism, and psychopathy. These individuals don’t just want to win; they derive pleasure from the suffering of others. For them, the game isn’t about the chicken dinner; it’s about the salt they harvest from their victims. Consalvo (2009) offers a broader typology of cheaters, including those who are stuck, those who want to “play God,” and those who are simply “jerks.” This groundbreaking work suggests that some people cheat not for any deep-seated psychological reason, but simply because they can. They are the digital equivalent of the person who double-dips at a party: they know it’s wrong, they just don’t care.

Finally, there is the economic perspective. The rise of professional gaming and streaming has turned victory into a viable career path. In this high-stakes environment, cheating is not just about ego; it’s about economics. A win can mean more subscribers, bigger sponsorships, and a lucrative career. This has given rise to a sophisticated black market for cheats, where illicit software is sold for top dollar. The literature on this topic reads like a spy novel, with tales of reverse-engineered anti-cheat software, encrypted forums, and international syndicates of code-savvy criminals. The battle royale is not just a game; it’s an economy, and where there’s money, there’s always a shortcut.

## ANALYSIS

To truly understand why battle royales are such a fertile breeding ground for cheaters, we must dissect the very fabric of the genre. It is a confluence of game design, human psychology, and the unique social dynamics of a 100-player deathmatch that creates this perfect storm of digital malfeasance. In this section, we will put on our metaphorical lab coats and peer into the petri dish of the battle royale, analyzing the various factors that contribute to the cheating epidemic.

At its core, the battle royale is a game of extreme variance. The random nature of loot drops, the unpredictable movement of the circle, and the sheer chaos of 99 other players mean that even the most skilled player can be eliminated by a stroke of bad luck. A player might spend 20 minutes looting, only to be unceremoniously dispatched by someone who happened to find a better weapon or a more advantageous position. This high degree of randomness can be intensely frustrating. For some, cheating is a way to mitigate this variance, to impose order on a chaotic system. An aimbot doesn’t care about your bad luck; it just cares about headshots. This is the “control freak” cheater, the one who can’t handle the inherent unfairness of a random number generator and decides to implement their own, more predictable, system of justice.

Furthermore, the massive scale of a 100-player game creates a sense of anonymity and detachment. In a smaller, more intimate game, players are more likely to form social bonds and a sense of community. Cheating in such an environment would be a social faux pas, a betrayal of trust. In a battle royale, however, your 99 opponents are just faceless avatars, digital lemmings to be herded off a cliff. This dehumanization makes it much easier to justify cheating. It’s not that you’re ruining someone’s day; you’re just… winning. This is the “it’s just a game” cheater, the one who conveniently forgets that on the other end of that internet connection is a real person who just threw their keyboard across the room in a fit of rage.

The high stakes of the “last one standing” model also play a significant role. The singular focus on victory, the all-or-nothing nature of the game, creates an immense pressure to win. This is exacerbated by the social status and, in some cases, financial rewards associated with victory. For many, winning is not just about personal satisfaction; it’s about social currency. A high win rate, a coveted in-game rank, or a popular stream can all be powerful motivators. When the pressure to win becomes overwhelming, the temptation to take a shortcut can be irresistible. This is the “win at all costs” cheater, the one who sees the game not as a form of entertainment, but as a ladder to be climbed, by any means necessary.

Finally, we cannot ignore the role of the cheating industry itself. The market for cheats is a multi-million dollar enterprise, with sophisticated software, dedicated customer support, and even subscription models. This professionalization of cheating has lowered the barrier to entry. You no longer need to be a coding genius to cheat; you just need a credit card and a questionable moral compass. The availability and ease of use of these cheats create a vicious cycle: the more people cheat, the more others feel they need to cheat to compete, which in turn fuels the demand for more cheats. This is the “if you can’t beat ‘em, join ‘em” cheater, the one who has been pushed to the dark side by the sheer prevalence of their cheating brethren.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The theoretical and psychological underpinnings of cheating are all well and good, but to truly appreciate the absurdity and audacity of the phenomenon, we must turn to the real world. The annals of battle royale history are littered with tales of cheaters, from the comically inept to the criminally sophisticated. These stories are not just cautionary tales; they are a window into the soul of the digital rogue.

One of the most infamous examples of cheating in a battle royale comes from the world of professional *Fortnite*. In 2019, two players, known by their online handles “X” and “Y,” were caught colluding during the *Fortnite* World Cup qualifiers. They were found to be secretly communicating and coordinating their actions, a clear violation of the rules. The community was outraged, not just because of the cheating itself, but because of the sheer arrogance of doing it on such a public stage. This was not some anonymous player in a random lobby; these were professionals, with careers and reputations on the line. The incident served as a stark reminder that even at the highest levels of competition, the temptation to cheat can be too strong to resist.

Another classic case study is the rise and fall of the “undetectable” cheat. For a brief, glorious period, a new cheat for *Apex Legends* was making the rounds. It was marketed as being completely undetectable by the game’s anti-cheat software, and for a while, it was. Players who used it were untouchable, racking up impossible win streaks and leaving a trail of frustrated opponents in their wake. The forums were ablaze with accusations, but the cheaters remained smug and secure. Then, one day, the hammer fell. The game’s developers, in a move of beautiful, karmic justice, had been secretly flagging the accounts of the cheaters. They didn’t ban them immediately; they let them continue to play, to invest their time and money, to build up their ill-gotten ranks. Then, in a single, glorious wave, they banned them all. The schadenfreude was palpable. The forums erupted in celebration. It was a digital day of reckoning, a reminder that the house always wins.

Of course, not all cheating is so dramatic. For every high-profile professional caught with their hand in the digital cookie jar, there are thousands of everyday players using more subtle, less detectable cheats. The use of “macros,” for example, is a gray area that has been the subject of much debate. A macro is a script that automates a series of actions, such as building a complex structure in *Fortnite* with a single keypress. Is it cheating? Is it just a clever use of game mechanics? The answer depends on who you ask. The developers say it’s cheating. The players who use them say it’s just being efficient. The players who get killed by them say it’s a one-way ticket to a special circle of hell reserved for people who talk in the theater.

These examples, from the brazen to the banal, illustrate the diverse and complex nature of cheating in battle royales. They show that cheating is not a monolithic phenomenon, but a spectrum of behaviors, driven by a variety of motivations. They also show that the battle against cheating is a never-ending arms race, a constant cat-and-mouse game between developers and the ever-resourceful community of digital deviants.

## DISCUSSION

The rampant cheating in battle royale games is more than just a nuisance for legitimate players; it is a fascinating case study in human behavior, with implications that extend far beyond the digital battlefield. By examining the motivations and methods of cheaters, we can gain a deeper understanding of consumer habits, the nature of competition, and the ever-shifting definition of fairness in a digital age.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the cheating phenomenon highlights the powerful allure of the “quick fix.” In a world of instant gratification, the idea of slowly and painstakingly developing a skill can seem… quaint. Why spend hundreds of hours practicing your aim when you can download a program that does it for you? This is the same impulse that drives people to buy fad diets, get-rich-quick schemes, and other products that promise maximum results with minimum effort. The cheating industry is, in essence, a service that caters to this desire for a shortcut to success. It is a testament to the fact that, for a certain segment of the population, the destination is more important than the journey, especially if the journey involves getting repeatedly headshot by a 12-year-old with more free time than you.

The battle royale also serves as a microcosm of our increasingly competitive society. The winner-take-all mentality that pervades so much of modern life is mirrored in the game’s design. In such an environment, the pressure to succeed can be immense, and the line between acceptable and unacceptable behavior can become blurred. Cheating, in this context, can be seen as a rational, if unethical, response to an irrational system. If the only thing that matters is winning, then any action that leads to victory can be justified. This raises uncomfortable questions about our own values. Are we a society that values fair play and sportsmanship, or are we a society that values winning above all else? The answer, if the prevalence of cheating in battle royales is any indication, is not as clear-cut as we might like to think.

Finally, the debate over what constitutes cheating in the first place speaks to the evolving nature of fairness in a digital world. Is using a high-refresh-rate monitor cheating? Is using a gaming mouse with programmable buttons cheating? Is using a third-party program to optimize your network connection cheating? The lines are constantly being redrawn, as technology and player ingenuity continue to push the boundaries of what is possible. This is not just a debate for gamers; it is a debate for society as a whole. As our lives become increasingly intertwined with technology, we will be forced to confront these same questions in a variety of contexts, from the classroom to the workplace. The battle royale, in its own chaotic and often hilarious way, is simply the canary in the coal mine.

## CONCLUSION

In the grand, chaotic theatre of the battle royale, where 100 souls vie for a fleeting moment of digital glory, the cheater stands as a testament to the enduring, and often infuriating, complexities of human nature. We have journeyed through the psychological swamps of inadequacy, the sociological minefields of hyper-competition, and the economic back-alleys of the cheating black market. We have seen that cheating is not simply the domain of the malicious or the lazy, but a multifaceted phenomenon with roots that run deep into the very design of the games themselves and the society that plays them.

The battle royale, with its intoxicating blend of high stakes, anonymity, and sheer, unadulterated chaos, creates an environment where the temptation to cheat is not just present, but pervasive. It is a world where the line between skill and software is constantly being blurred, and where the desire for victory can eclipse the principles of fair play. The battle against cheating is a never-ending arms race, a digital dance between developers and deviants that is likely to continue as long as there are games to be played and egos to be bruised.

So, what have we learned from this journey into the heart of digital darkness? We have learned that people will go to extraordinary lengths to win, even when the prize is nothing more than a virtual chicken dinner. We have learned that the desire for a shortcut is a powerful and profitable force. And we have learned that, for all our technological advancements, the human capacity for both ingenuity and ignobility remains as boundless as ever. The next time you are unceremoniously eliminated from a battle royale by a player who seems to defy the laws of physics, take a moment to reflect on the profound, and profoundly absurd, human drama that is playing out on your screen. Then, by all means, report them.
references:
Consalvo, M. (2009). *Cheating: Gaining advantage in videogames*. MIT press.

Lee, S. J., & Lee, S. J. (2021). Why do some users become enticed to cheating in competitive online games? An empirical study of cheating focused on competitive motivation, self-esteem, and aggression. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *12*, 768825.

Lee, S. J., & Lee, S. J. (2023). The influence of psychological needs and motivation on game cheating: insights from self-determination theory. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *14*, 1278738.

Von Stroheim, K. (1986). *God Mode and Its Discontents*. Fictional Press.

A. Nonymous. (2023). Personal communication.
